archetype: Submarine Cable Fault Localization and Repair Orchestration System
description: This system coordinates the end-to-end repair workflow for fiber-optic submarine cables after a fault is detected, integrating real-time acoustic positioning of Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs), dynamic permitting across Exclusive Economic Zones (EEZs), and cable ship logistics to minimize Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) in a high-stakes, legally constrained maritime environment.
rules: MUST integrate acoustic positioning data (e.g., from USBL transponders) and apply sound velocity profiles to correct ROV location in real-time, per industry standards like ISO 19683.
MUST enforce permit and notification workflows governed by UNCLOS and coastal state regulations, with geofenced compliance checks against maritime claim polygons.
MUST process and correlate raw telemetry from submarine line terminal equipment (SLTE) using ITU-T Rec. G.997.2 to pinpoint fault location within cable sections.
MUST interface with legacy ROV control systems via proprietary serial protocols (e.g., Sperry Marine) for tooling commands and sensor feeds.
MUST model and schedule cable ship mobilization considering fuel bunkering, weather windows (via GRIB data), and ROV operational depth limits.
anti_patterns: DO NOT treat it as a generic ticketing system—failing to model the legal adjacency of EEZs and continental shelf claims will cause illegal deployments and international incidents.
DO NOT ignore acoustic positioning uncertainty—uncorrected sound channel variations can misposition ROVs by tens of meters, causing missed graves and failed retrievals.
DO NOT assume static repair schedules—ignoring tidal currents and shipping lane traffic in the fault area will lead to vessel collisions or grounding.
key_technical_challenges: Fusing noisy, high-frequency acoustic positioning data with low-latency ship GNSS and dynamic oceanographic models (currents, thermoclines) in a unified coordinate system.
Building a deterministic permit engine that can translate complex, non-digital coastal state agreements into executable geospatial rules for vessel and ROV operations.
Achieving interoperability with decades-old, vendor-specific ROV control systems that lack modern APIs, requiring protocol reverse-engineering and hardware-in-the-loop simulation.

recommended_stack.language: C++ (for real-time acoustic data processing and ROV control integration) with Rust for safety-critical permit validation logic.
recommended_stack.framework: Node.js with TypeScript for workflow orchestration and multi-tenant UI, leveraging its async I/O for concurrent maritime stakeholder comms.
recommended_stack.database_engine: TimescaleDB for high-frequency time-series acoustic and vessel telemetry, with PostGIS for complex maritime boundary queries.
recommended_stack.cache: Redis for low-latency caching of vessel positions, permit status, and real-time weather/current grid data.
recommended_stack.message_broker: RabbitMQ with MQTT plugin for reliable, ordered telemetry ingestion from at-sea assets over satellite links.
special_services: Acoustic Positioning Correction Microservice (using BELLHOP ray-tracing models)
Maritime Regulatory Graph Database (UNCLOS/IMO rules as property graphs)

compliance_flags: UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea)
Coastal State National Regulations (e.g., US NOAA permits)
